Learn React #19 — Spinning Up a Project with Create React App (CRA)

This kicks off a brand new chapter — the Create React App (CRA) chapter. From here on, we'll slowly move away from CodePen and start working in the kind of development environment you'd actually use day to day on real projects.
CRA is short for Create React App — same vibe as VAR being short for Vue, Angular, React (just grab the capital letters).

CRA gets a development environment ready for you really fast. If you've ever used Vue CLI, the concept is exactly the same. The nice thing about CRA is that you don't have to learn Webpack from scratch. Setting up a working dev environment by hand-rolling Webpack yourself is incredibly time-consuming — not to mention you'd have to learn Webpack and a whole pile of other stuff first.

So how do you actually use CRA? Before we get into that — if this is your first time and you haven't installed Node.js yet, I'd suggest installing it. CRA is built on top of Node.js, so you need Node.js installed before you can use CRA.
I'd also recommend checking out these posts of mine to set up nvm:
Installing and using nvm to switch Node.js versions on Windows 10
Installing nvm on Mac OS X
In short, nvm is a Node.js version manager. In real-world development you'll switch Node.js versions all the time, so it's a good idea to install and use nvm.
And here are some common nvm troubleshooting posts for reference:
Fixing "Access is denied" or "exit status 145" with nvm on Windows
How to fix "npm WARN config global --global, --local are deprecated. Use --location=global instead"
Fixing "command not found: npm" when running commands on Mac
"The term 'XXX' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or executable program"
Hopefully that helps you get nvm installed and running smoothly.

Ok to proceed? (y)

Don't worry, just go ahead and hit
. It's only telling you it needs to install the
package. After that you'll need to wait a bit — anywhere from a few seconds to a few minutes, depending entirely on your network.

This post feels a little short, so let me also cover the CRA project structure to round it out.

Generally speaking, we'll be working with files under

, because CRA looks for that folder by default.
Now you might be thinking, "Wait, how does this project compile without any obvious tooling? Is it using Webpack? I don't see any config files anywhere?"
You're right — under the hood, CRA does use Webpack. And just like Vue CLI, all the Webpack stuff lives under
. If you're curious, take a peek inside
— that's where CRA's Webpack config lives. I won't go into it here.

So really, you already recognize at least 80% of the code in here. In the next post, I'll walk through the remaining 20% you haven't seen yet.
